claimed_contribution: Provides a historical assessment of Harper W. Boyd Jr.'s editorship and its role in maintaining JMR relevance during a period of disciplinary change.
Offers a more fine-grained Boyd-era topical profile of published JMR content using annual subject index counts.
Adds an article-level citation ranking for the Boyd years to identify publications with lasting influence.
context: The paper is a historical retrospective on the Journal of Marketing Research during November 1975 to August 1978, when Harper W. Boyd Jr. served as editor. It situates this period in a marketing discipline that was becoming more specialized, with new conferences and journals emerging.
design_and_data: This is a descriptive archival essay based on JMR publication records and citation counts. The author reports that the 12 Boyd-era issues contained roughly 110 articles and 90 research notes and communications, plus special issue/section content. For topical prevalence, the essay uses JMR annual subject index entries (the same input source referenced in Huber, Kamakura, and Mela 2014) to tabulate the most common topics. For influence, the author conducted a Google Scholar citation analysis in May 2013 covering all full-length articles, research notes, and communications, excluding book reviews and computer abstracts.
main_findings: The Boyd years are characterized as one of JMR's most innovative periods, with new prominence of econometrics, survey design, sales force, and segmentation alongside established consumer behavior and advertising topics.

Boyd-era content was broad across theory, methods, and substantive issues, but publication attention was concentrated in a limited set of topics, while product, price, services marketing, and customer satisfaction were not among leading topics.
In the 2013 citation audit, Armstrong and Overton (1977) was a dominant outlier, with more than eight times the citations of the second-most-cited Boyd-era article.
one_line_summary: This essay reviews Harper W. Boyd Jr.'s 1975-1978 JMR editorship by documenting what was published and what remained influential in later citations.
relation_to_prior_work: synthesizes prior JMR historical discussion with Boyd-era descriptive evidence by pairing topic index tabulations with article-level citation outcomes.
research_gap: Huber, Kamakura, and Mela (2014) provide a broader topical history of JMR, but the Boyd period lacked a more fine-grained descriptive portrait and an explicit article-level impact summary in this retrospective context. This essay addresses that need with detailed topic counts and citation rankings for the Boyd years.
research_question: What characterized the mix of topics published in JMR during Harper W. Boyd Jr.'s editorship, and which Boyd-era publications had the strongest long-run impact? The essay also examines how this editorial period reflected broader changes in the marketing discipline.
